title: Geographic Considerations in Evaluating Women’s Employment Opportunities 2026 – World Bank E-Learning Course on Gender, Geography, and Employment Access
summary: The Geographic Considerations in Evaluating Women’s Employment Opportunities 2026 course is an innovative e-learning program developed to deepen understanding of how geography and spatial environments influence women’s access to employment and economic opportunities. This course introduces learners to the Geospatial Women’s Employment Analytical Framework (GeoWEAF) , a cutting-edge tool developed by the World Bank Group. It explores how environmental, social, and infrastructural factors either enable or restrict women’s participation in the labor market. Through interactive videos, readings, quizzes, and discussions, participants gain practical insights into the relationship between gender equality and geographic conditions shaping employment opportunities. This training is part of a broader effort to promote inclusive development, gender equality, and evidence-based policymaking in employment systems globally. About the Course This e-learning program is designed to help participants understand how location and environment influence women’s ability to access jobs, education, transport, healthcare, and financial services. It also introduces analytical tools and datasets used in modern gender and spatial analysis. The course is structured to be flexible, accessible, and practical, making it suitable for professionals and students interested in gender, development, and urban planning. Key characteristics of the course include: Fully online e-learning format Duration of approximately 2 hours Multiple video-based lessons Text materials and downloadable resources Interactive quizzes and assessments Online discussion forums Certification upon successful completion Course Objectives By the end of this course, participants will be able to: Understand the relationship between geography and gender in employment access Explore how environmental conditions influence women’s economic opportunities Learn the GeoWEAF analytical framework developed for gender and spatial analysis Identify and use open and proprietary data sources for gender-based spatial evaluation Understand the Gender Enabling Environments Spatial Tool (GEEST) and its applications in policymaking Course Structure and Modules The course is divided into six structured learning sections: 1. Geography and Gender Nexus This module introduces the connection between geography and women’s employment opportunities. Topics include: Introduction to GeoWEAF framework Geographic barriers affecting women’s employment Career access challenges based on location Pre-course assessment and introductory materials 2. Contextual Dimension This module explores institutional and structural factors influencing women’s employment. Key areas include: Workplace discrimination Regulatory frameworks affecting gender equality Financial inclusion and access to economic resources 3. Accessibility Dimension This module focuses on access to essential services that support employment. Topics include: Women’s mobility and travel patterns Access to public transportation systems Access to education and training institutions Access to healthcare and financial services 4. Place-Characterization Dimension This module examines environmental and social conditions shaping women’s opportunities. Key elements include: Safety and security conditions Conflict and fragility environments Education levels and digital inclusion Environmental hazards and water/sanitation access 5. Spatial Data and Analysis This module introduces data-driven approaches to gender and geography. Topics include: Spatial data collection and interpretation Data sources for accessibility analysis Contextual and environmental data evaluation Application of geospatial tools in policymaking 6. Closing Module The final section includes: Final assessment and evaluation Post-course assessment Feedback submission Certificate issuance Acknowledgments and course completion What’s Included in the Course Participants will benefit from a structured learning experience including: Video lectures and expert presentations Text-based learning materials Interactive quizzes and assessments Discussion forums for peer learning Official certification upon completion Learning Outcomes and Benefits This course provides strong professional and academic value, especially for individuals working in gender, development, and policy-related fields. Key benefits include: Strong understanding of gender and geography intersection in employment Practical knowledge of spatial and gender analytical tools Enhanced skills in data interpretation and policy analysis Improved ability to design gender-inclusive development strategies Professional certification from a recognized global institution Exposure to World Bank research methodologies and frameworks Target Audience This course is designed for: Urban planners and city development professionals Gender specialists and equality advocates Policymakers and government officials Researchers and academics Students in social sciences, development, and geography Professionals working in social development and employment policy It is also suitable for anyone interested in understanding how geography affects women’s economic empowerment. Duration and Format Total duration: 2 hours Format: Online e-learning Content: Videos, texts, quizzes, forums Certification: Available upon completion Enrollment Information Enrollment is currently open. Course access begins on June 16, 2026 for registered participants. Participants are encouraged to enroll early to secure access and prepare for the learning start date. Conclusion The Geographic Considerations in Evaluating Women’s Employment Opportunities 2026 course offers a unique opportunity to explore the intersection of gender, geography, and employment through a scientific and data-driven lens. By combining theory, practical tools, and global frameworks like GeoWEAF, this course empowers learners to better understand and address the spatial barriers affecting women’s economic participation worldwide. It is a valuable learning resource for anyone committed to advancing gender equality, inclusive development, and evidence-based policymaking. Apply here Learn More For more information about this opportunity, Click here More global Training Program opportunities for youth, visit the OFY website: Click here The post Geographic Considerations in Evaluating Women’s Employment Opportunities 2026 – World Bank E-Learning Course on Gender, Geography, and Employment Access appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: GAYO Botswana Volunteer and Internship Opportunities 2026 – Call for Applications in Environmental Sustainability, Climate Action, and Youth Leadership Development
summary: The Green Africa Youth Organization Botswana (GAYO Botswana) has officially opened applications for its 2026 Volunteer and Internship Programme. This opportunity is designed for passionate young people who are eager to contribute to environmental sustainability, climate action, zero waste initiatives, and youth empowerment across Botswana and beyond. GAYO Botswana is a youth-led environmental organization that focuses on building a generation of environmentally conscious leaders. Through its programs, the organization promotes climate justice, environmental education, community engagement, and sustainable development practices. This call for applications is an excellent opportunity for individuals seeking practical experience, mentorship, and exposure in the environmental and climate change sector. About GAYO Botswana GAYO Botswana operates under the broader vision of the Green Africa Youth Organization network, which is dedicated to: Promoting climate action and environmental sustainability Supporting youth participation in environmental governance Advancing zero waste and circular economy solutions Building climate-resilient communities Encouraging environmental education through eco-clubs and youth networks The organization empowers young people to become active contributors to environmental solutions through volunteering, internships, advocacy, and grassroots engagement. Available Opportunities GAYO Botswana is currently recruiting for both volunteer and internship positions. All roles are designed to provide learning, professional development, and hands-on experience in environmental work. Volunteer Positions (Unpaid) These roles are strictly voluntary and focus on building experience in environmental and organizational work. Zero Waste Volunteer (1 Position) Responsibilities include: Supporting zero waste initiatives and awareness campaigns Assisting in community education on waste reduction and recycling Helping design and implement sustainability programs Administrative Volunteer (1 Position) Responsibilities include: Supporting day-to-day organizational operations Managing documentation and internal coordination tasks Assisting with scheduling and communication activities Communications Volunteer (1 Position) Responsibilities include: Supporting social media and digital communications Creating awareness content on environmental issues Assisting in storytelling and campaign visibility Eco-Clubs Volunteer (1 Position) Responsibilities include: Supporting school and community eco-club programs Assisting in environmental education activities Engaging youth in sustainability projects and training sessions Internship Position Youth Climate Council (YCC) Intern (1 Position) This internship is a structured learning opportunity designed for individuals interested in climate policy, advocacy, and youth engagement. Key focus areas include: Climate change policy and governance exposure Youth advocacy and leadership development Stakeholder engagement and environmental networking Participation in climate-related programs and discussions This role is ideal for individuals who want to build a career in environmental policy, sustainability, or development work. Important Notes All volunteer positions are unpaid and intended for learning and professional development. The Youth Climate Council (YCC) Internship is also a learning-focused opportunity, not a paid position. Only shortlisted candidates will be contacted after application review. Applicants are required to submit: Updated CV (PDF format) Cover Letter (PDF format) Eligibility Requirements Applicants are expected to meet the following criteria: Strong interest in environmental sustainability and climate action Willingness to learn and contribute to volunteer-based initiatives Good communication and teamwork skills Ability to commit time to the selected role Passion for youth development and community engagement Application Process Applicants are required to submit their documents through the official application process (Google Form as provided by GAYO Botswana). Required documents: Updated CV (PDF) Cover Letter (PDF) Important confirmation: Applicants must acknowledge that they understand the roles are unpaid and primarily focused on learning and professional development. Contact email provided for inquiries: konediakaridia499@gmail.com Why This Opportunity Matters This program offers valuable exposure for young people interested in environmental work. Participants will gain: Practical experience in real environmental projects Exposure to climate change and sustainability initiatives Networking opportunities with youth and environmental professionals Skills development in communication, administration, and advocacy A foundation for future careers in environmental and development sectors Deadline The application deadline has not been specified in the available announcement. Applicants are encouraged to apply as early as possible due to limited positions. Conclusion The GAYO Botswana Volunteer and Internship Programme 2026 is a powerful opportunity for young environmental enthusiasts to contribute to meaningful climate action while gaining essential professional experience. Whether through communications, administration, zero waste initiatives, eco-club engagement, or climate policy exposure, this program offers a platform for impactful youth involvement in environmental sustainability. Apply here Learn More For more information about this opportunity, Click here More global Internship opportunities for youth, visit the OFY website: Click here The post GAYO Botswana Volunteer and Internship Opportunities 2026 – Call for Applications in Environmental Sustainability, Climate Action, and Youth Leadership Development appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: MultiLAwa 2026 Fully Funded Marie Skłodowska-Curie Double Degree PhD Fellowships in Europe | 14 Open Positions for International Researchers
summary: The MultiLAwa Doctoral Network has officially opened applications for 14 fully funded Marie Skłodowska-Curie Double Degree PhD Fellowships under the Horizon Europe MSCA Doctoral Networks – Joint Doctorates programme. This prestigious European doctoral initiative is designed to train the next generation of multilingual and interdisciplinary researchers for the digital and Artificial Intelligence (AI) era. The programme, known as MultiLAwa (Multilingual Language Awareness in the European Digital Society), offers an exceptional opportunity for international students and early-career researchers who are passionate about linguistics, multilingualism, AI, digital communication, language technologies, sociolinguistics, and related fields. Successful candidates will receive full funding, competitive salaries, international mobility opportunities, joint doctoral degrees from two European universities, and advanced interdisciplinary training across several countries in Europe. The application deadline is 8 July 2026 at 11:59 pm (CEST). About the MultiLAwa Doctoral Network MultiLAwa is a Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions (MSCA) Joint Doctorate programme funded under Horizon Europe. The initiative aims to prepare doctoral researchers for emerging professional demands linked to digital transformation, multilingual communication, and Generative AI technologies. The programme focuses on developing experts in: Language awareness Multilingual communication AI-driven language technologies Computational linguistics Digital linguistics Sociolinguistics Gender linguistics Lexicography Terminology Language policy Language teaching and didactics The consortium also integrates expertise from other scientific and professional sectors, including: Finance and Economic Sciences Information Science and Engineering Artificial Intelligence and Multimedia Environmental Sciences Circular Economy This broad interdisciplinary approach ensures that Doctoral Candidates gain the skills necessary to thrive in academic, governmental, technological, educational, and industrial environments. What Makes the MultiLAwa PhD Fellowship Unique? The MultiLAwa programme is not a traditional PhD programme. It is a highly international and interdisciplinary doctoral training network designed around multilingualism, mobility, and collaboration. 1. Double Degree PhD Structure All selected Doctoral Candidates (DCs) will receive: One joint doctoral degree (“cotutelle”) Two PhD diplomas from two universities in different countries Candidates will study and conduct research at multiple institutions across Europe. 2. Fully Funded Fellowship All positions are fully funded for 36 months under the MSCA regulations. The funding package includes: Monthly living allowance: EUR 4010 (gross) Monthly mobility allowance: EUR 710 Monthly family allowance: EUR 660 (if applicable) Long-term leave allowance (if applicable) Special needs allowance (if applicable) Salary amounts will vary according to country correction coefficients. 3. International Research Mobility Doctoral Candidates will: Spend time at two host universities Participate in international secondments Conduct research stays ranging from a few days to six months Collaborate with partner organisations and industries across Europe 4. Multilingual and Interdisciplinary Environment The programme promotes plurilingualism and multicultural collaboration. Internal communication and training activities will be conducted in both: English German Researchers will also benefit from exposure to additional European languages and cultures. 5. Strong Career Development Support Each Doctoral Candidate will receive: A Personal Career Development Plan (PCDP) Professional mentoring Transferable skills training Participation in conferences and workshops Public engagement opportunities Advanced research training Research Areas Covered by MultiLAwa The consortium combines expertise in several cutting-edge disciplines, including: Linguistics and Language Studies Applied Linguistics Sociolinguistics Gender Linguistics Interactional Linguistics Lexicography Terminology Language for Specific Purposes (LSP) Didactics Language Policy Digital and AI Fields Digital Linguistics Computational Linguistics Natural Language Processing (NLP) Artificial Intelligence Language Technologies Interdisciplinary Areas Multimedia and Information Science Finance and Economics Environmental Sciences Circular Economy Host Countries and Salary Correction Coefficients The fellowship includes placements across multiple European countries. Country correction coefficients apply to the living allowance. 2025 Country Correction Coefficients Austria: 109.7% Denmark: 132.8% France: 118.1% Germany: 101.2% Italy: 95.3% Poland: 74.1% Portugal: 93.7% Switzerland: 163.8% Eligibility Criteria for the MultiLAwa Fellowship To apply for the programme, applicants must satisfy all eligibility requirements. Applicants Must: 1. Not Hold a Doctoral Degree Candidates must not already possess a PhD at the time of recruitment. Even candidates who have defended their thesis but have not yet officially received their degree are not eligible. 2. Hold a Relevant Master’s Degree Applicants must possess one of the following: Master of Arts Master of Translation Master of Education Master of Science (MSc) Equivalent qualification The degree must be in: Linguistics Translation Studies Language Studies Or a closely related discipline Applicants must also demonstrate strong academic performance with distinction-level grades. Minimum Academic Standards Examples include: UK: Above 70% France: Above 14/20 Germany/Austria: Better than 2 (“gut”) Switzerland: Above 5.5 Denmark: Above 7 3. Meet the MSCA Mobility Rule Applicants must not have: Lived Worked Studied in the recruiting university’s country for more than 12 months during the last 36 months before recruitment. 4. Demonstrate Strong Language Skills Applicants must have at least: B2 level German B2 level English Language certificates or equivalent evidence must be provided. Knowledge of additional languages is considered an advantage. 5. Be Interested in International and Interdisciplinary Research Candidates should: Enjoy multicultural collaboration Be willing to travel internationally Participate in research secondments Work in interdisciplinary environments What Training Will Fellows Receive? Selected Doctoral Candidates will participate in a highly structured doctoral training programme. Training opportunities include: Research methodology workshops AI and language technology training Academic writing development Public speaking and dissemination training Transferable skills courses International conferences Collaborative projects Public engagement activities Online Information Session for Applicants An online information session will be organized for interested applicants. Information Session Details Date: Tuesday 16 June 2026 Time: 2:00 pm (CEST) Format: Online The participation link will be shared later by the organizers. Fellowship Duration and Start Date Duration 36 months full-time employment contract Start Date 1 November 2026 Why This Fellowship Matters in the AI and Digital Era The growing influence of Artificial Intelligence, digital communication, multilingual societies, and language technologies has created increasing demand for experts who understand both human language and digital systems. MultiLAwa responds directly to this demand by training researchers capable of: Bridging linguistic and technological fields Supporting multilingual digital societies Developing ethical and inclusive AI communication systems Advancing research in language technologies and cultural communication The programme is particularly valuable for students interested in careers in: Academia AI and NLP industries European institutions Language policy Translation technologies International organisations Education Research institutes Application Deadline The deadline to apply for the MultiLAwa 2026 Marie Skłodowska-Curie Double Degree PhD Fellowships is: 8 July 2026 at 11:59 pm (CEST) Applicants are encouraged to prepare their applications early and attend the online information session. How to Apply Interested candidates should apply through the official MultiLAwa recruitment portal. Apply here For more information on this PhD fellowship visit here Discover more fellowship opportunities on OFY, click here The post MultiLAwa 2026 Fully Funded Marie Skłodowska-Curie Double Degree PhD Fellowships in Europe | 14 Open Positions for International Researchers appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: ADB AI for Safer Roads Innovation Challenge 2026: Call for AI Experts, Data Scientists, and Transport Innovators Across Asia and the Pacific
summary: Road traffic injuries remain one of the leading causes of death globally, particularly in low- and middle-income countries where infrastructure, speed management systems, and urban planning often struggle to keep pace with rapid development and population growth. Across Asia and the Pacific, governments and development institutions are increasingly seeking data-driven approaches to improve road safety and reduce preventable deaths. To address this urgent challenge, Asian Development Bank has launched the AI for Safer Roads Innovation Challenge 2026 , an international innovation competition designed to harness artificial intelligence, mobility data, and geospatial analysis to improve road safety systems across the region. The initiative is being implemented in collaboration with the World Bank Development Impact Group, AI for Good, and International Telecommunication Union, with support from JFPR and HLTF. The challenge invites data scientists, AI specialists, transport engineers, policy innovators, researchers, and multidisciplinary teams from ADB member countries to develop innovative AI-powered models capable of identifying where speed limits may not align with real-world road conditions and safety needs. About the AI for Safer Roads Innovation Challenge The AI for Safer Roads Innovation Challenge aims to explore how emerging technologies can transform the way governments and institutions understand and manage road safety risks. By combining: Artificial Intelligence (AI) Large-scale mobility datasets GPS probe data Geospatial analysis Machine learning tools Road network information Street-level imagery the challenge seeks to uncover hidden risk patterns and generate practical solutions that can help policymakers make evidence-based decisions on road safety interventions. Unlike traditional traffic monitoring systems that focus on whether drivers exceed speed limits, this challenge specifically asks participants to determine whether the posted speed limits themselves are appropriate for the actual road environment. Core Challenge Question Participants are expected to answer the following central question: How might AI and mobility data be used to determine where speed limits are misaligned with real-world road conditions, thereby supporting evidence-based speed management across Asia and the Pacific? This focus aligns with global Safe System principles, which emphasize designing transport systems that reduce the likelihood of fatal and serious injuries. Objectives of the Competition The competition aims to support the development of analytical tools and scalable methodologies that can: Assess whether posted speed limits align with Safe System principles Detect road segments where current speed limits may increase risk for vulnerable road users Generate spatial outputs and geospatial visualizations Help governments prioritize safety interventions Support scalable, data-driven transport policy decisions Improve evidence-based road safety management Who Should Apply? ADB is encouraging participation from experts and innovators with backgrounds in: Artificial Intelligence Data Science Transport Engineering Urban Planning Geospatial Analysis Public Policy Machine Learning Mobility Analytics Road Safety Research Teams can consist of: Individual participants Small multidisciplinary teams Groups of up to five people The competition encourages collaborative problem-solving that combines technical expertise with policy understanding. Key Features Required in Submitted Solutions Participants must design analytical models capable of performing several critical functions. 1. Safe Speed Assessment Teams should develop methodologies that assess whether posted speed limits are appropriate based on: Road function Operating speeds Land use patterns Traffic intensity Urban or rural classifications The objective is to evaluate whether speed limits support safe mobility for all road users. 2. Risk Identification Solutions should identify road segments where current speed limits may expose vulnerable users to elevated risks. This includes areas affecting: Pedestrians Cyclists Powered two-wheeler users Communities near schools or markets Participants are encouraged to use AI and data analysis techniques to uncover safety vulnerabilities not immediately visible through conventional assessments. 3. Policy-Ready Outputs One of the most important aspects of the challenge is the production of practical outputs governments can use directly. Solutions should therefore generate: A Speed Safety Score Geospatial visualizations Interactive mapping outputs Priority intervention recommendations These outputs should support policymakers in identifying high-risk road segments for review or redesign. Datasets Provided to Participants Registered participants who agree to the Non-Disclosure Agreement (NDA) will receive access to anonymized and aggregated datasets. Available Datasets Include: GPS Probe Data This dataset includes: Operating speeds 85th percentile speeds Speed distributions Posted speed limits Traffic intensity indicators Road Network Data Participants will also receive road infrastructure data such as: Functional road classifications Urban/rural segmentation Intersection density Road segment lengths Mapillary Street-Level Imagery Street-level imagery datasets will provide: Crowdsourced road images Machine-learning identified road features Road sign information Optional Contextual Layers Additional contextual datasets may include: Population density Land use patterns School proximity indicators Market proximity Powered two-wheeler indicators These datasets are intended to support comprehensive AI-driven analysis. Timeline of the Competition Application Phase Deadline: 25 June 2026 Participants must submit: AI analytical models Geospatial analyses Supporting documentation before the deadline. Review Phase July 2026 Expert reviewers will evaluate all submissions. The top five solutions will be shortlisted and officially announced in September 2026. Refinement Phase September 2026 Shortlisted teams will: Build visualizations on ADB’s GIS platform Refine their solutions Incorporate technical feedback from reviewers and experts Final Pitch Phase October 2026 Finalists will present their solutions before a high-level jury panel. This stage provides teams with the opportunity to showcase the practical impact and scalability of their innovation. Why This Challenge Matters Road crashes claim millions of lives globally every year, with vulnerable road users disproportionately affected. Many existing road safety systems rely on outdated methods or incomplete data when determining appropriate speed limits. By integrating AI, mobility analytics, and geospatial intelligence, this initiative seeks to modernize road safety decision-making. The challenge contributes to broader goals including: Safer urban mobility Sustainable transportation systems Evidence-based policymaking Reduced road fatalities Improved infrastructure planning Data-driven governance It also demonstrates the growing role of AI in solving complex public-sector and development challenges. Benefits of Participating Participants in the AI for Safer Roads Innovation Challenge can gain several advantages: International visibility Exposure to global development institutions Networking with leading AI and transport experts Opportunity to contribute to road safety innovation Potential integration into regional policy discussions Experience working with large-scale mobility and geospatial datasets Recognition from ADB and international partners Shortlisted teams will also have the opportunity to refine and showcase their solutions on ADB’s GIS platform. Application Deadline The deadline to submit solutions is: 25 June 2026 Applicants are encouraged to register and begin working with the datasets as early as possible. Official Website For full details and registration, visit: ADB Challenges Official Platform Apply here Learn More For more information about this opportunity, Click here More global Competition opportunities for youth, visit the OFY website: Click here The post ADB AI for Safer Roads Innovation Challenge 2026: Call for AI Experts, Data Scientists, and Transport Innovators Across Asia and the Pacific appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Global Adolescent Health Short Course 2026 at LSHTM: Apply for the International Training Program on Adolescent Health, Policy, and Development
summary: Applications are now open for the 2026 Global Adolescent Health Short Course offered by the London School of Hygiene Tropical Medicine (LSHTM) in collaboration with the World Health Organization (WHO), UNICEF, Obafemi Awolowo University, the Institute of Child Health at the University of Ibadan, and the Public Health Foundation of India. This internationally recognized short course is designed to strengthen the capacity of professionals working in adolescent health and development by equipping them with evidence-based tools, conceptual frameworks, and policy approaches necessary to improve adolescent health systems worldwide. The course will run from 7 September to 18 September 2026 and will be delivered in a hybrid format, allowing participants to join either face-to-face in London or online from anywhere in the world. With growing concerns around adolescent mental health, sexual and reproductive health, violence, nutrition, substance use, infectious diseases, non-communicable diseases, and digital wellbeing, this course provides participants with advanced practical and policy-oriented training to address some of the most pressing public health challenges affecting adolescents globally. Why Adolescent Health Matters Investing in adolescent health is critical for achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), improving public health outcomes, and promoting sustainable economic development. Adolescence is a transformative stage of life where behaviors, attitudes, and social patterns are established. Many adult diseases and health conditions — including HIV, cardiovascular diseases, lung cancer, mental health disorders, and substance abuse — often originate from behaviors developed during adolescence. Despite increasing global evidence on effective interventions for adolescent health, many countries still face challenges related to limited resources, weak health systems, inadequate policies, and insufficient technical expertise. The Global Adolescent Health Course seeks to bridge these gaps by building the skills of policymakers, researchers, program implementers, and public health professionals. About the Global Adolescent Health Course The Global Adolescent Health course is jointly organized and taught by leading experts from: London School of Hygiene Tropical Medicine (LSHTM) World Health Organization (WHO) Obafemi Awolowo University, Nigeria Institute of Child Health, University of Ibadan Public Health Foundation of India UNICEF The course combines public health theory, evidence-based practice, and policy analysis to prepare professionals to design and implement effective adolescent health programs. Course Dates Course Period: 7 – 18 September 2026 Duration: 10 days Learning Mode: Hybrid (Online and Face-to-Face) Location for In-Person Participants: London, United Kingdom Application Deadline: Friday, 7 August 2026 at 23:59 (UK Time) Objectives of the Course The course aims to equip participants with the knowledge and practical skills needed to improve adolescent health policies and interventions. Participants will learn to: Understand the epidemiology of adolescent health issues Analyze evidence-based interventions for adolescent wellbeing Evaluate health systems and adolescent health programs Design and improve adolescent health policies and strategies Conduct research and evidence reviews related to adolescent health Understand behavioral theories, vulnerability, resilience, and risk frameworks Apply adolescent participation and youth engagement approaches Integrate adolescent health into national public health systems Topics Covered in the Program Participants will explore a wide range of adolescent health and development topics, including: Core Themes Adolescent development and wellbeing Epidemiology of adolescent health Risk and resilience theories Youth engagement and participation Intersectional perspectives in adolescent health Adolescent rights and diversity Health programming for adolescents Digital-age adolescent wellbeing Public Health Challenges The course addresses major adolescent health challenges such as: Sexual and reproductive health Mental health Nutrition Drugs and alcohol Violence and injuries Infectious diseases Non-communicable diseases Policy and Research Areas Participants will also learn about: Situation analysis Monitoring and evaluation Health surveillance systems Research methods for adolescent health Policy development and implementation International priorities and frameworks for adolescent health Teaching Methods and Learning Experience The course will be taught through: Interactive lectures Synchronous online sessions Face-to-face learning Pre-recorded case studies Group projects Participatory discussions Evidence review exercises Participants will receive electronic access to: PowerPoint presentations Course materials Key academic references Supporting resources The course faculty includes internationally recognized experts in adolescent health programming, research, and policy development. Who Should Apply? The course is specifically designed for mid-career professionals involved in adolescent health and development. Suitable applicants include: Public health professionals Policymakers Researchers Program implementers NGO professionals Development practitioners Health sector managers International organization staff Applicants should have: Experience in adolescent health or related fields Strong interest in public health and development Good command of English The course is public health-oriented rather than clinically focused. Intended Learning Outcomes By the end of the course, participants will be able to: Critically evaluate adolescent health indicators and burden measures Apply research evidence to policy and programming Assess health system capacities for adolescent interventions Analyze policy effectiveness at different governance levels Strengthen adolescent health strategies in their countries and organizations Testimonials from Previous Participants Past participants have described the course as transformative and highly practical. Professionals from organizations such as UNICEF, UNFPA, UNESCO, ministries of health, and international NGOs praised the course for: Its participatory teaching style Practical policy relevance Strong evidence-based approach Real-world applications Networking opportunities Global perspectives on adolescent wellbeing Course Fees for 2026 Standard Fee £1,932 Discounted Fee £966 for nationals of Low- and Middle-Income Countries (LMICs) currently residing in LMICs Applicants requesting the discounted fee must provide: Passport copy Proof of current residence Evidence of LMIC status Please note that no scholarships or fee waivers are available for the 2026 intake. Important Information for Applicants Application Deadline Applications close on: Friday, 7 August 2026 at 23:59 (UK Time) Applicants are strongly encouraged to apply early because the course may close before the official deadline if spaces become full. Visa Information Participants attending in person are responsible for obtaining the appropriate UK visa. LSHTM can provide supporting documentation if required. Accommodation LSHTM provides accommodation guidance and hotel recommendations for participants attending physically in London. Additional Notes Participants must bring their own laptops Passport identification is mandatory for registration Participants requiring visas should begin the process early LSHTM may cancel the course if enrollment numbers are insufficient Apply Here Why You Should Apply This course offers a rare opportunity to gain advanced global expertise in adolescent health from some of the world’s leading institutions in public health and development. Participants will benefit from: International networking opportunities Practical policy and programming skills Exposure to global adolescent health experts Evidence-based learning approaches Hybrid learning flexibility Real-world case studies and research Professionals working in public health, youth development, education, international development, and health policy will find this course highly valuable for career growth and program impact. For professionals seeking to strengthen adolescent health systems and improve outcomes for young people globally, this course provides an exceptional learning opportunity. For more information on this short course, click here Discover more short course opportunities on OFY here The post Global Adolescent Health Short Course 2026 at LSHTM: Apply for the International Training Program on Adolescent Health, Policy, and Development appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Interledger Fellowship Program 2026 | $92,000 Funding for Digital Financial Inclusion Projects
summary: Applications are now officially open for the 2026 Interledger Fellowship Program, a prestigious global fellowship supporting innovators, researchers, developers, educators, advocates, and creators working to remove barriers to digital financial inclusion. The Interledger Foundation Fellowship is a fully funded 12-month program designed to support individuals building solutions that help underserved communities access and participate meaningfully in digital financial systems. Selected fellows will receive a total compensation package of up to $92,000, including direct fellowship funding and project support. Applications will remain open until June 15, 2026, at 11:59 PM EDT. About the Interledger Fellowship Program The Interledger Fellowship Program is a global initiative focused on advancing equitable access to digital financial systems and economic participation. The fellowship supports individuals whose work addresses real-world financial exclusion challenges, including: High transaction costs Documentation barriers Limited financial literacy Exclusionary regulations Inaccessible digital infrastructure Lack of community-centered financial tools The program seeks to empower fellows who are developing innovative approaches that make financial systems more inclusive, accessible, and community-driven. Fellowship Duration The fellowship runs for: 12 months During this period, fellows will work on their proposed projects while engaging with the Interledger Foundation’s global ecosystem. Funding and Benefits Selected fellows will receive: Fellowship Compensation $72,000 total fellowship compensation Project Support Funding Additional $20,000 stipend for: Research Development Organizing Advocacy Community engagement Technical implementation Total Funding Up to $92,000 overall support This funding is intended to allow fellows to focus fully on impactful projects that advance financial inclusion and digital participation. Focus Areas for 2026 The Interledger Foundation is seeking proposals in several critical areas related to digital financial inclusion. 1. Research Projects that study: Regulatory barriers Economic challenges Technical obstacles Social exclusion in digital finance systems The fellowship welcomes evidence-based research exploring why certain populations remain excluded from digital financial services. 2. Education and Capacity Building Projects that: Create educational resources Develop financial literacy programs Build community training initiatives Increase awareness of digital financial tools This area is especially focused on communities with limited exposure to digital financial technologies. 3. Policy and Standards Projects advocating for: Inclusive regulatory frameworks Open financial systems Technical interoperability standards Consumer protection without exclusion Applicants working on governance, policy reform, and open standards are encouraged to apply. 4. Solutions for Underserved Markets The fellowship supports innovators creating: Financial tools Digital payment systems Open-source technologies Accessible infrastructure Community-centered financial products Projects should focus on populations currently underserved by existing systems. 5. Ecosystem Strengthening Projects that build: Communities Networks Support systems Collaborative platforms The goal is to help people navigate and benefit from digital financial services more effectively. Who Should Apply? The fellowship welcomes diverse applicants from across sectors and disciplines. Ideal applicants include: Researchers Software developers Open-source contributors Educators Community organizers Entrepreneurs Policy experts Financial inclusion advocates Artists and storytellers Human rights advocates Digital rights activists The program especially values applicants working closely with underserved communities. Why This Fellowship Matters Digital financial systems increasingly shape how people: Access opportunities Receive payments Build businesses Participate in society Yet millions remain excluded due to systemic barriers. The Interledger Fellowship aims to support people building more equitable systems where access to financial tools is: Affordable Inclusive Community-centered Privacy-conscious Accessible to all The program recognizes that financial inclusion is not only about technology, but also about dignity, participation, and economic justice. Inspiration from Previous Fellows The fellowship builds on the success of the Interledger Ambassadorship Program, which since 2020 has supported global innovators working in: Open-source technology Accessibility Advocacy Education Research Community engagement Past fellows have explored themes including: Digital equity Financial rights Data governance Accessibility in technology Community-centered finance Diaspora financial systems Inclusive digital infrastructure Application Timeline Applications Open April 1, 2026 Application Deadline June 15, 2026 11:59 PM EDT Late applications may not be accepted. How to Apply Applications must be submitted through the official Submittable platform. Applicants should review: The FAQ The official Application Guide Eligibility requirements Proposal instructions before applying. APPLY HERE Why You Should Apply The Interledger Fellowship is one of the most impactful global opportunities for innovators working at the intersection of: Technology Finance Community development Human rights Open systems Economic inclusion The fellowship provides: Significant funding support Global visibility Access to an international network Space to focus on impactful work Opportunities to shape the future of digital finance For researchers, developers, educators, and advocates committed to equitable economic participation, this fellowship offers both resources and a powerful platform. For more information on this fellowship visit here For more Fellowship Opportunities, visit OFY here. The post Interledger Fellowship Program 2026 | $92,000 Funding for Digital Financial Inclusion Projects appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Social Fiction Design Competition 2026: Win USD 10,000 by Imagining a Better World for Young People in 2050
summary: Young writers, dreamers, storytellers, and changemakers from around the world are invited to participate in the Social Fiction Design Competition 2026, a global creative writing competition that challenges participants to imagine a better, fairer, and more sustainable world for young people in the year 2050. Organized by Yunus Center and powered through YY Ventures , this international initiative encourages people everywhere to use the power of imagination and storytelling to envision solutions to today’s social and environmental challenges. The competition is open globally with no age restrictions and no geographical limitations, making it one of the most inclusive international creative writing opportunities currently available. Participants stand a chance to win a grand prize of USD 10,000 while contributing to conversations about the future of humanity, sustainability, innovation, and social transformation. What is the Social Fiction Design Competition? The Social Fiction Design Competition is an international writing competition focused on “Social Fiction” — a form of imaginative storytelling similar to science fiction, but centered on social innovation and human progress rather than futuristic technology alone. The competition invites participants to imagine: What the world could look like in 2050 How young people may live in the future How societies can overcome inequality and injustice What sustainable communities might become How governments, businesses, and communities can work together for positive change The goal is to inspire new ways of thinking about social innovation and encourage people to imagine the world they truly want to build. According to Nobel Peace Laureate Muhammad Yunus : “If we do not imagine, it will not be done. If we imagine, someday it will happen.” This powerful idea forms the foundation of the competition. What is Social Fiction? Social Fiction is a storytelling approach that imagines a future society shaped by positive social transformation. Unlike traditional science fiction, which often focuses heavily on futuristic machines or scientific advancement, Social Fiction focuses on: Human-centered innovation Social justice Sustainability Equality Community transformation Ethical development Inclusive economies Environmental healing The concept encourages writers to creatively explore solutions to real-world problems and imagine societies where communities thrive. The organizers believe that imagination can inspire real-world innovation and policy change. Competition Theme: The Life of a Young Person in 2050 Participants are asked to imagine and describe: “How do you wish to see the life of a young person in the year 2050?” Writers are encouraged to take readers on a journey into a future world that is free from the social and environmental injustices affecting communities today. The competition emphasizes optimism, innovation, and bold thinking. Applicants should imagine a future where systems, institutions, and communities have evolved to create better opportunities and improved quality of life for younger generations. Writing Requirements Participants must submit an original Social Fiction piece between: Minimum: 1,000 words Maximum: 5,000 words The writing should creatively explore a fictional world in 2050 while addressing one or more major social or environmental issues. Suggested Focus Areas Participants may choose to focus on one or several of the following areas: Climate Change Agriculture Technology and Innovation Micro-credit Transport Literature Music Waste Management Circular Economy Tourism WASH (Water, Sanitation, and Hygiene) Employment Sports Health and Well-being Community Development Sustainability Social Equity The organizers encourage participants to think broadly and creatively about how these sectors could evolve positively by 2050. What Should Your Story Include? The competition encourages writers to explain how different stakeholders contribute to building a better future. Stories may include the role of: Governments Educational institutions Corporate organizations Local businesses Community leaders Entrepreneurs Youth movements Civil society organizations Participants should demonstrate how collaboration and innovation help shape a more just and sustainable society. Why This Competition Matters The Social Fiction Design Competition is not just a writing contest. It is a platform for global imagination and social innovation. At a time when the world faces challenges such as: Climate change Rising inequality Unemployment Environmental degradation Mental health concerns Economic instability Social exclusion The competition seeks bold visions of hope and possibility. It encourages people to move beyond criticism and begin imagining actionable futures worth building. Prize Opportunity: Win USD 10,000 One of the major attractions of the competition is the opportunity to win: USD 10,000 The prize recognizes outstanding creativity, originality, optimism, and social imagination. Beyond financial rewards, participants gain international exposure and become part of a global conversation on innovation and sustainable futures. Who Can Participate? One of the strongest features of the Social Fiction Design Competition is its accessibility. There are: No age restrictions No geographical limitations This means anyone from anywhere in the world can participate. Students, writers, professionals, activists, researchers, entrepreneurs, and aspiring storytellers are all encouraged to apply. Can You Apply More Than Once? According to the competition FAQ: Multiple applications are not permitted. Applicants are encouraged to focus on submitting one strong and well-developed Social Fiction piece. Is There an Application Fee? The competition states that there is: No application fee Participation is completely free. Can Teams Apply? The FAQ section indicates that applicants should review the application guidelines regarding team participation. Participants are encouraged to verify current submission rules through the official competition platform. Previous Winners and Finalists The competition has already attracted talented participants from different countries around the world. Previous Winner Amit Kumar Mishra – Nepal Previous Finalists Neha Sabu – India Wan Faizal Mohd – Malaysia Anne Julienne Barcelona – United States Tyfur Rahman – Bangladesh The diversity of past finalists demonstrates the truly international nature of the competition. Why You Should Apply Participating in the Social Fiction Design Competition can help writers and changemakers: Showcase creativity globally Build visibility as a thought leader Contribute to social innovation conversations Inspire real-world solutions Develop storytelling and writing skills Gain recognition for visionary ideas Connect imagination with impact For young innovators, students, activists, and creative thinkers, this is a unique opportunity to influence future thinking through storytelling. How to Apply Applicants are required to: Write an original Social Fiction piece Ensure the submission falls within 1,000–5,000 words Complete the online application form Submit the story through the official competition platform For inquiries: Email: info@yy.ventures WhatsApp: +880 1648-397420 Apply Here For more information on this competition visit here Discover more opportunities on OFY here The post Social Fiction Design Competition 2026: Win USD 10,000 by Imagining a Better World for Young People in 2050 appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Global AgriInno Challenge 2026: Fully Funded International Opportunity for Agrifood Innovators Solving Climate and Food Challenges in Small Island Developing States
summary: The Global AgriInno Challenge (GAC) 2026 is now officially open for applications, offering an exceptional international platform for startups, agripreneurs, researchers, and innovation teams developing transformative agrifood solutions for Small Island Developing States (SIDS). Co-organized by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations , Zhejiang University , and Pinduoduo , the Global AgriInno Challenge has become one of the world’s leading youth-focused agrifood innovation competitions. The initiative connects emerging innovators with investors, policymakers, researchers, global experts, and technical partners to accelerate impactful solutions for sustainable food systems. The 2026 edition specifically focuses on Small Island Developing States (SIDS), which face increasing threats from climate change, food insecurity, biodiversity loss, geographic isolation, and limited innovation ecosystems. Despite these vulnerabilities, many SIDS remain underserved in global investment and technology opportunities, making this challenge particularly significant. Applications are open globally until 20 June 2026 (24:00 CEST) . What is the Global AgriInno Challenge 2026? The Global AgriInno Challenge is an international innovation competition designed to identify, support, and scale technology-driven agrifood solutions that can strengthen resilience and sustainability in vulnerable island regions. The challenge seeks innovations capable of transforming food systems while addressing pressing environmental, economic, and social issues affecting island communities worldwide. GAC 2026 emphasizes both “blue” and “green” agrifood systems, encouraging solutions related to: Agriculture Fisheries Aquaculture Climate resilience Sustainable food production Nutrition Biodiversity conservation Rural livelihoods Youth employment Digital agriculture The competition creates opportunities for innovators to gain international visibility, receive mentorship and coaching, network with industry leaders, and pitch their solutions on a global stage. Why the Focus on Small Island Developing States (SIDS)? Small Island Developing States are among the regions most vulnerable to: Rising sea levels Extreme weather events Climate-related disasters Dependence on imported food Limited agricultural land Fragile ecosystems Economic vulnerability Youth unemployment Weak innovation infrastructure At the same time, SIDS possess enormous potential for sustainable innovation, climate-smart agriculture, fisheries transformation, renewable systems, and community-based development. The Global AgriInno Challenge aims to support practical innovations that respond directly to the realities of island life and create long-term resilience for local communities. Challenge Themes for Global AgriInno Challenge 2026 Applicants must develop or scale solutions aligned with at least one of the four key pillars critical to SIDS resilience. 1. Better Production This pillar focuses on strengthening food production systems despite environmental and geographic limitations. Innovations may address: Limited arable land Climate pressures on agriculture Fisheries sustainability Food-import dependency Fragmented supply chains Precision agriculture Smart farming technologies Sustainable aquaculture Digital farming systems Solutions that improve productivity while promoting sustainability are highly encouraged. 2. Better Nutrition This theme focuses on improving access to affordable, healthy, and nutritious diets. Applicants may propose innovations that tackle: Malnutrition Obesity Diet-related non-communicable diseases (NCDs) Food affordability challenges Nutrition education Food quality Local food systems The challenge encourages scalable approaches that can improve public health outcomes in island communities. 3. Better Environment Environmental protection is a major priority for SIDS due to their highly vulnerable ecosystems. Relevant innovation areas include: Coral reef protection Coastal ecosystem restoration Biodiversity conservation Climate adaptation Sustainable resource management Forest conservation Ocean sustainability Climate-smart technologies Solutions should demonstrate measurable environmental impact and long-term sustainability. 4. Better Life This pillar focuses on improving livelihoods and inclusive economic development. Applications may address: Rural development Youth unemployment Gender inclusion Digital inclusion Economic empowerment Entrepreneurship Job creation Community resilience The challenge seeks innovations capable of improving quality of life and expanding opportunities for underserved populations. Who Can Apply for the Global AgriInno Challenge 2026? Applications are open to agripreneurs and innovation teams from around the world. To qualify, applicants must meet the following eligibility requirements: Eligibility Requirements Applicants must: Be registered as a company or legal entity. Have less than USD 1,000,000 in annual turnover. Have operated for less than 5 years. Possess a technology-based solution or business model at Technology Readiness Level (TRL) 7–9. Provide evidence that the innovation is beyond the MVP (Minimum Viable Product) stage. Have at least one team leader under the age of 35. Commit to active participation throughout the entire programme. Have a solution directly addressing agrifood challenges in SIDS or already piloted in island environments. Important Notes Teams from outside SIDS are fully eligible to apply. Preference will be given to teams originating from or led by individuals from SIDS. Teams with female agripreneurs are strongly encouraged to apply. Only one application per applicant will be accepted. The final submission will be considered the official application. Programme Activities and International Exposure Selected teams will participate in several international activities designed to strengthen their innovations and visibility. Innovation for Good Workshops Finalists will receive coaching, mentorship, and capacity-building support through specialized workshops focused on innovation scaling, pitching, and agrifood entrepreneurship. Global Final Pitch Event in Hangzhou, China Finalists will travel to Hangzhou, China in August 2026 to present their solutions before international experts, investors, policymakers, and stakeholders. This stage offers substantial global exposure and networking opportunities. FAO Science and Innovation Forum Showcase in Rome, Italy Outstanding teams may also be showcased during the FAO Science and Innovation Forum in Rome in October 2026, further increasing international recognition and partnership opportunities. Key Benefits for Finalists and Winners Participants selected for the Global AgriInno Challenge 2026 can expect numerous benefits, including: Global visibility and recognition International networking opportunities Access to investors and technical experts Mentorship and coaching support Participation in global innovation workshops Exposure to policymakers and development partners Opportunities for collaboration and partnerships Inclusion in long-term alumni activities Potential international showcase opportunities The challenge provides a strong platform for scaling innovative agrifood solutions globally. Working Languages The official working languages for GAC 2026 are: English Chinese However: All proposals must be submitted in English. All presentations must also be conducted in English. Global AgriInno Challenge 2026 Timeline Key Dates Date Activity 19 May – 20 June 2026 Open Call for Applications 18 June – 30 June 2026 Preliminary Round Selection 2 July 2026 Announcement of Top 13 Finalists July 2026 Remote Preparation and Coaching August 2026 Innovation for Good Workshops and Global Final Pitch Event in Hangzhou 12 – 16 October 2026 FAO Science and Innovation Forum Showcase in Rome 2027 onwards Alumni Network Activities Why Agrifood Startups Should Apply The Global AgriInno Challenge is more than a competition. It is a strategic opportunity for young innovators to gain access to: International markets Global investors Policy stakeholders Technical mentorship International recognition High-level innovation ecosystems For startups working in climate-smart agriculture, food systems, aquaculture, fisheries, rural livelihoods, nutrition, environmental sustainability, and digital agriculture, this challenge offers a unique platform to scale impact globally. Innovators already working in African island contexts, Caribbean nations, Pacific islands, and other vulnerable coastal environments may find this opportunity especially relevant. Data Protection and Privacy According to the organizers, FAO processes all personal and non-personal data in line with its internal Data Protection Policy and procedures. The organization emphasizes: Fair data processing Transparency Security Integrity Responsible handling of information Applicants are encouraged to review the official privacy policies before submitting applications. How to Apply Interested applicants should review the official application requirements and FAQs carefully before submission. Applications close on: Deadline: 20 June 2026 (24:00 CEST) For further inquiries, applicants may contact: global_agriinno_challenge@zju.edu.cn e-agriculture@fao.org Apply Here For more information on the competition, visit here Discover more opportunities on OFY, click here The post Global AgriInno Challenge 2026: Fully Funded International Opportunity for Agrifood Innovators Solving Climate and Food Challenges in Small Island Developing States appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: NASA Creator Proposals 2026: Opportunity for Filmmakers, Storytellers, Songwriters, Poets and Documentarians to Partner with NASA
summary: NASA has opened an exciting opportunity for creative professionals who want to help tell the stories behind some of the agency’s most ambitious missions and innovations. As NASA continues to push the boundaries of space exploration, aviation research, nuclear propulsion, and lunar missions, the agency is inviting creators to submit proposals for potential partnerships that will help bring these mission stories to wider audiences. This opportunity is designed for filmmakers, documentarians, songwriters, storytellers, poets, and other creative professionals who can develop powerful content around NASA’s work. Through this call, NASA is looking for partners who can communicate the agency’s missions in compelling, accessible, educational, and inspiring ways. NASA published the Announcement for Proposals on May 21 and is inviting eligible creators to submit their proposals by Tuesday, June 30 . About the NASA Creator Proposals Opportunity The NASA Creator Proposals opportunity is part of the agency’s wider effort to share the human stories, technical achievements, scientific goals, and future possibilities connected to its missions. NASA is currently working on major programs that could shape the future of space exploration and aviation. These include the Artemis Moon missions, the development of future lunar infrastructure, nuclear propulsion technologies, missions connected to Mars exploration, and advanced aeronautics research. Through this opportunity, NASA wants creative partners who can help translate these complex missions into stories that the public can understand, appreciate, and engage with. In this initial round, NASA is seeking up to 10 partners through unfunded Space Act Agreements . These partnerships will allow selected creators to work with NASA in sharing stories, insights, and educational content related to selected agency missions and programs. What NASA Is Looking For NASA is looking for creators who can present strong storytelling ideas connected to the agency’s missions and innovation areas. Eligible creative professionals may include: Filmmakers Documentarians Songwriters Storytellers Poets Creative producers Multimedia creators Other content creators with strong storytelling proposals The agency is especially interested in proposals that can help communicate the stories behind NASA’s missions, not just the technical details. This means applicants should think about how their creative work can explain the significance, challenges, human effort, innovation, and future impact of NASA’s work. NASA Mission Areas Covered by This Opportunity NASA has identified several areas of focus for this opportunity. Applicants can submit proposals connected to one or more of these mission areas. 1. Artemis Program and Future Moon Missions One of the major focus areas is NASA’s Artemis program. The Artemis program is NASA’s effort to return humans to the Moon and prepare for future deep space exploration. Through Artemis, NASA aims to establish a long-term human presence on and around the Moon while developing the knowledge, technologies, and systems needed for future missions to Mars. The opportunity mentions several Artemis-related areas, including: The Artemis program as a whole The recently added Artemis III mission in 2027 The Artemis IV lunar landing in 2028 NASA’s plans to develop a Moon Base Broader stories connected to lunar exploration and future human presence on the Moon Creators interested in space exploration, human achievement, science communication, lunar missions, and the future of humanity beyond Earth may find this area especially suitable. 2. Nuclear Propulsion and Mars Exploration NASA is also inviting proposals connected to its advancement of nuclear propulsion. Nuclear propulsion is an important area of future space exploration because it could support faster and more efficient travel beyond Earth orbit. This technology could play a key role in future missions to Mars and other deep space destinations. The announcement specifically mentions NASA’s nuclear propulsion work, including: The Space Reactor-1 Freedom mission to Mars in 2028 The Skyfall payload Broader innovation in nuclear propulsion for future space missions This focus area may be ideal for creators interested in futuristic technology, engineering, Mars exploration, science storytelling, and the next generation of space travel. 3. NASA Aeronautics and Aviation Innovation NASA’s work is not limited to space exploration. The agency is also involved in cutting-edge aviation research and aeronautics innovation. Through flight tests and other aviation-focused efforts, NASA continues to contribute to the future of air travel, aircraft performance, flight safety, sustainability, and advanced aviation technology. Creators may submit proposals focused on NASA’s aviation work, including: Flight tests Aeronautics research Advanced aircraft concepts Innovation in aviation technology Stories behind NASA’s work in improving air travel and flight systems This area may appeal to creators interested in aviation, engineering, technology, climate-conscious transport innovation, and the future of flight. Nature of the Partnership Selected creators will enter into unfunded Space Act Agreements with NASA. This means the opportunity is a partnership arrangement rather than a traditional funded grant. NASA is not offering direct funding under this opportunity. Instead, selected partners may receive collaboration opportunities that could include access to NASA expertise, facilities, personnel, mission insights, or other support depending on the approved proposal and NASA’s needs. Applicants should clearly explain what kind of support they need from NASA to carry out their project. Possible needs may include: Access to NASA facilities Access to NASA personnel Interviews with mission teams Technical background information Mission-related insights Coordination support Visual, educational, or archival resources where available Other specific support required to complete the proposed creative work Applicants should also clearly explain their own funding and distribution plans. What Proposals Should Include NASA has stated that proposals should include clear details about the applicant’s area of focus, funding arrangements, distribution approach, and specific needs from the agency. A strong proposal should likely explain: The mission area of interest Applicants should state whether they want to focus on Artemis, Moon missions, nuclear propulsion, Mars-related innovation, aeronautics, or another NASA mission area. The creative concept Applicants should describe the story they want to tell and why it matters. The format of the project This may include a film, documentary, song, poetry project, storytelling series, digital media project, educational content, or another creative format. The intended audience Applicants should explain who the project is designed for, such as students, general audiences, science enthusiasts, educators, families, young people, or global audiences. Funding arrangements Since this is an unfunded partnership, applicants should explain how the project will be financed or supported. Distribution arrangements Applicants should explain how the final creative work will reach audiences. This may include film festivals, streaming platforms, social media, schools, museums, public events, media outlets, or digital platforms. Specific support needed from NASA Applicants should be clear about any access, interviews, facilities, personnel, or mission information they need from NASA. Production timeline A clear timeline can help NASA understand whether the project is realistic and achievable. Experience and capacity Applicants should show that they have the skills, creative experience, or team capacity needed to deliver the proposed project. Who Can Apply? This opportunity is primarily focused on U.S. creators . However, NASA has indicated that it will consider proposals that include a minority of international participants . This means international creators may be included as part of a proposal, but the opportunity is mainly intended for U.S.-based creators or U.S.-led creative teams. Applicants should review the full requirements carefully before submitting. Why This Opportunity Matters NASA’s missions are some of the most important scientific and technological efforts in the world. However, these missions are not only about rockets, aircraft, spacecraft, engineering systems, or scientific instruments. They are also about people, imagination, discovery, risk, innovation, and the future of humanity. Creative professionals play an important role in helping the public connect emotionally and intellectually with science and exploration. Through storytelling, film, music, poetry, documentary work, and multimedia content, creators can help audiences understand: Why NASA’s missions matter How space exploration benefits humanity What challenges scientists and engineers are solving How aviation and space technology affect everyday life What the future of the Moon, Mars, and deep space exploration may look like How young people can be inspired to pursue science, technology, engineering, arts, and mathematics This opportunity gives creators a chance to help shape how the public experiences and understands NASA’s next chapter. Deadline for Applications The deadline to submit proposals is Tuesday, June 30 . Interested applicants are encouraged to review the full requirements and prepare a detailed proposal before the deadline. Application Link Full requirements and application details are available here: https://go.nasa.gov/CreatorProposals Contact Information For further information, applicants may contact: Camille Gallo NASA Headquarters, Washington Phone: 202-358-1600 Email: camille.m.gallo@nasa.gov Cheryl Warner NASA Headquarters, Washington Phone: 202-358-1600 Email: cheryl.m.warner@nasa.gov Final Thoughts The NASA Creator Proposals 2026 opportunity is a unique chance for creative professionals to partner with one of the world’s leading space and aeronautics agencies. Whether through film, documentary, music, poetry, storytelling, or multimedia production, selected creators will have the opportunity to help communicate the stories behind NASA’s missions and innovations. For creators passionate about space exploration, science communication, aviation, the Moon, Mars, and the future of humanity, this opportunity offers a powerful platform to contribute to public understanding and inspiration. Applicants should prepare strong, clear, and realistic proposals that explain their creative vision, distribution plan, funding arrangements, and the support they would need from NASA. Deadline: Tuesday, June 30 APPLY HERE. For more information about this opportunity click here. Discover more proposal writing opportunities on OFY here. The post NASA Creator Proposals 2026: Opportunity for Filmmakers, Storytellers, Songwriters, Poets and Documentarians to Partner with NASA appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .

title: Postdoctoral Research Fellow in Quantitative Political Science at the University of Oxford (Voter ID Project)
summary: The University of Oxford invites applications for the prestigious role of Postdoctoral Research Fellow in Quantitative Political Science , contributing to the ESRC‑funded Voter ID Project led by Principal Investigator Prof Petra Schleiter . This full‑time, fixed‑term position offers an exceptional opportunity to join one of the world’s most respected political science research environments and contribute to cutting‑edge work on electoral behaviour, voter identification policies, and democratic participation in the United Kingdom. This role is based within the Department of Politics and International Relations (DPIR) , one of the largest and most influential political science departments globally. The successful candidate will work closely with a distinguished research team, including co‑investigators Prof Margit Tavits and Prof Jonathan Homola , and will engage deeply with experimental survey design, advanced quantitative analysis, and policy‑relevant research dissemination. Application Deadline: 12 noon (UK time) on Friday, 26 June 2026 About the Voter ID Project The ESRC‑funded Voter ID Project investigates the effects of introducing mandatory photo identification in the UK—a country without national ID cards. The research integrates multiple data sources and methodologies to understand how voter ID requirements shape political behaviour, attitudes, and participation. The project includes three major components: Original surveys with embedded experiments Designed to measure behavioural and attitudinal responses to voter ID requirements. Historical data from Northern Ireland Examining the introduction of voter ID in the early 2000s. Data from the English voter ID trials (2018–2019) Providing comparative insights into turnout, postal voting, and demographic impacts. A new and significant strand of the project focuses on young voters , including those aged 16–17 , to understand how voter ID policies influence the youngest cohort of current and future voters. Key Responsibilities The Postdoctoral Research Fellow will play a central role across all strands of the project. Responsibilities include: 1. Experimental Research Design and Survey Management Contributing to the design of experimental research. Coding and testing survey instruments. Monitoring data collection and quotas in collaboration with survey firms. Conducting data quality checks. 2. Methodological Development Adapting existing methodologies and developing new ones tailored to the project’s data. Advising team members on methodological and procedural matters. 3. Data Analysis Analysing original survey data. Working with historical voter ID data from Northern Ireland. Examining turnout, postal voting, and related electoral datasets. 4. Dissemination and Policy Engagement Presenting findings at conferences. Contributing to world‑class publications. Preparing policy briefs. Supporting engagement with policymakers, including: The Electoral Commission The Department for Housing, Levelling Up and Communities House of Commons Committee Clerks 5. Project Administration Assisting with grant applications. Organising workshops and conferences. Managing personal research activities and workload. 6. Required Training Upon Appointment New staff must complete training in: Information Security and Data Protection Implicit Bias Bullying and Harassment Equality and Diversity Harassment in Higher Education Selection Criteria Essential Qualifications and Experience Applicants must demonstrate: A completed or near‑completed doctorate in political science or a related field. Experience with survey experiments, survey design, and survey data analysis. Expertise in advanced quantitative methods and research design. Proficiency in R . Strong understanding of UK electoral politics. Ability to publish in leading political science journals. Strong communication and interpersonal skills. Commitment to equality, diversity, and inclusion. Evidence of good citizenship and willingness to contribute to administrative tasks. Ability to critically evaluate recent trends in the field. Desirable Attributes Experience collaborating on research articles for publication. About the University of Oxford The University of Oxford is a global leader in research and education, known for its commitment to innovation, academic excellence, and societal impact. Oxford fosters a diverse, inclusive, and supportive environment where scholars thrive and contribute to global knowledge. The University is: A top institution for research excellence. A leader in entrepreneurship, producing 15–20 spin‑out companies annually. Home to world‑class cultural, academic, and professional resources. About the Social Sciences Division Oxford’s Social Sciences Division is a powerhouse of interdisciplinary research, supporting scholars through: Research development guidance Funding opportunities Project design and management support Career development resources About the Department of Politics and International Relations (DPIR) DPIR is one of the world’s most respected political science departments, ranked #1 in the UK by The Guardian (2024). With around 90 academic staff, the department hosts major research projects, postdoctoral researchers, and a vibrant academic community. The department is committed to: Equality, diversity, and inclusion Applying for the Athena Swan Bronze Award Supporting staff wellbeing Providing a collaborative and dynamic research environment Benefits of Working at Oxford Oxford offers an exceptional benefits package, including: 38 days of paid holiday Generous pension schemes Travel discounts Professional development opportunities Free entry to University museums and gardens Discounted access to sports facilities Childcare support and family‑friendly policies Staff networks for BME, LGBT+, disabled staff, and researchers Support for relocation and visa applications How to Apply Applications must be submitted through Oxford’s online recruitment portal. Applicants must upload: A CV A supporting statement addressing each selection criterion Details of two referees All documents must be submitted as PDFs. Deadline: 12 noon (UK time) on Friday, 26 June 2026 Apply here For more information about this opportunity visit here Discover more job opportunities here The post Postdoctoral Research Fellow in Quantitative Political Science at the University of Oxford (Voter ID Project) appeared first on Opportunities for Youth .
